vocal_direction.style: A slow-burning Americana-soul hybrid that lives in the liminal space between bluegrass intimacy and gospel-tinged warmth. The lead vocal should sound like a man sitting on a porch step at dusk, talking to no one and everyone — conversational grain in the verses that opens into full-chested, soulful pleading in the choruses. Nothing is oversold. Every note earns its place. The vocal performance is the sound of someone who knows this perfect moment is already slipping away, and that awareness makes it more beautiful, not less. Think of a voice weathered just enough to hold truth — the Appalachian twang of Dan Tyminski's vowels married to the round, warm chest resonance of Aloe Blacc's phrasing, never polished to a sheen but always on pitch where it matters. Let imperfection be the signature.
vocal_direction.lead_voice: Raw, aching bluegrass tenor sitting primarily in the A3–E4 range for verses, rising to E4–F#4 on chorus peaks, with a husky, slightly breathy low-mid delivery in intimate sections. The voice carries natural Appalachian coloring — open vowels that lean slightly nasal on words like 'screen,' 'means,' 'jars,' and 'chest,' but rounds into a warmer, more soulful placement (forward mask resonance, slight chest-mix) on chorus lines. A controlled bluegrass grain sits under the tone at all times — not gravel, but the texture of a voice that has lived outdoors. Vibrato is earned and restrained: a slow, natural wobble that only blooms on sustained words ('burning,' 'gold,' 'hold,' 'amen'). The singer should sound like he is 38 years old, has calluses on his hands, and is stunned by gratitude.
vocal_direction.emotional_delivery: Verse 1 is WONDER — quiet, wide-eyed, as if narrating a scene he can't believe is real. Each image (moths, singing, fireflies) should land with the gentleness of someone afraid that speaking too loudly will break the spell. Verse 2 shifts to TENDERNESS mixed with URGENCY — he sees his daughter, hears the radio, and the desire to stop time becomes almost physical; the line about pressing his thumb into the clock should carry a slight catch in the throat, a man wrestling with impermanence. The choruses are DEFIANT JOY — not aggressive, but the emotional equivalent of standing in a river and planting your feet. 'Let the good stay burning' is both a prayer and a command. The bridge is VULNERABLE RECKONING — future tense looking back, stripped bare, the voice thinning to its most exposed. 'Amen' is not religious performance; it is a man exhaling the truest word he knows, voice nearly cracking, a single syllable that contains an entire life's gratitude. The outro is BITTERSWEET RELEASE — past tense now ('we were golden'), the voice of someone smiling through tears, letting go not because he wants to but because he understands he must.
vocal_direction.phrasing: Verses: Conversational, unhurried phrasing that follows natural speech cadence rather than strict rhythmic grid. Breathe where you would breathe if you were telling someone this story on a porch — after 'screen' (line 1), after 'means' (line 2), let 'The kids are catching fireflies' run as one phrase with a micro-pause before 'in mason jars' to let the image land. The final two lines of each verse ('I got dirt on my hands… come to rest' and 'I wanna press my thumb… go ahead and stop') should be delivered as couplets with the second line slightly more forward in intensity. Use gentle consonant emphasis on plosives: 'Porch,' 'Pulling,' 'Peace,' 'Press' — these P-sounds are the heartbeat of the song. Choruses: Longer, more legato phrases. 'Let the good stay burning' should be one smooth arc with 'good' slightly emphasized and 'burning' carrying a slow bloom of vibrato. 'We're alive inside a moment that the years will ache to hold' is the longest phrase — take a preparatory breath after 'turning,' then commit to the full line, allowing 'ache' to be the emotional apex with a slight lean and thinning of tone. Bridge: Clipped, almost spoken phrasing on 'One day I'll close my eyes' — then 'and I'll be right back here' opens up with longer note values. 'The smoke, the laughter, and the sound of no fear' should be delivered as a list where each item gets its own weight, tiny pauses between like placing precious objects on a shelf. 'That I saw it — every bit of it — amen': the dashes are real silence, one beat each, isolating 'amen' completely. Outro: Ghostly legato. 'We were golden, we were golden' as a mantra, fading, the second repetition softer and slightly slower. Final 'And the good's still burning' barely above a whisper, the last word 'burning' allowed to trail off unresolved, like smoke.
vocal_direction.dynamics: The song follows a luminous arc — dawn to golden hour to twilight. INTRO/VERSE 1: pp to p. Voice close-mic'd, intimate, as if the singer's mouth is six inches from your ear. Dynamic range is narrow and restrained, hovering around piano. PRE-CHORUS 1: p to mp. A gentle swell begins — the voice gains 10-15% more chest engagement, vowels open slightly wider, as if the singer is straightening up from a lean. CHORUS 1: mp to mf. The voice blooms but deliberately holds back roughly 20% of its full power. This is a sunrise, not high noon. The loudest moment is 'years will ache to hold' before pulling back to mf for the tag ('the good's still burning gold'). VERSE 2: p to mp. Slightly fuller than Verse 1 from the start — the story is gaining momentum. The line about the clock pushes to mp with emotional intensity more than volume. PRE-CHORUS 2: mp building toward mf. More urgency than the first pre-chorus; the palm-muted strum underneath drives forward motion. CHORUS 2: mf to f. THIS IS THE PEAK. 'Let the good stay burning' hits with full-hearted, open-throated conviction. 'We're alive inside a moment' reaches the song's loudest vocal moment at forte, with 'ache' as the dynamic summit. 'And the good's still burning gold' sustains the power with a slow, controlled decrescendo into the bridge. BRIDGE: f drops sharply to pp. The dynamic cliff is intentional — from the most powerful moment to the most exposed. Voice and guitar only. Nearly whispered. 'Amen' is ppp — barely there, hanging in one beat of total silence. OUTRO: pp, rising gently to p on 'we were golden,' then decrescendo to ppp on the final 'still burning,' fading like embers.
vocal_direction.harmonies: Chorus 1: A single harmony voice enters on 'Let the good stay burning' — a high third (C#4 above the lead's A3), sung by a warm female or high male voice with a familial, slightly untrained quality. This voice doubles the lead melody a third above through 'Let the reel keep turning,' then drops out, returning on 'Let the porch light flicker' with a low fifth harmony below (a baritone voice, earthy and resonant). The three-part stack — low fifth, lead, high third — converges on 'And the good's still burning gold,' creating a brief, intimate family-choir moment that should sound like three people on a porch, not a studio vocal booth. Chorus 2: All three voices enter from the top of the chorus. The high third is slightly more prominent and confident. On 'We're alive inside a moment that the years will ache to hold,' add a fourth voice — a second female or countertenor singing a high fifth (E4) that enters on 'years' and sustains through 'hold,' creating a momentary four-part spread that is the harmonic climax of the song. The stack should swell and breathe together, not mechanically locked. Let the tuning be human — close but not auto-tuned perfect. Imperfections are warmth. Bridge: NO HARMONIES. Lead voice completely alone. This is sacred, solitary space. Outro: The high-third harmony returns on 'So let the good stay burning' — just two voices now, lead and one harmony, like two people left awake after everyone else has gone to bed. On the final, whispered 'And the good's still burning,' the harmony fades out one word before the lead, leaving the lead voice utterly alone on the word 'burning.' The last sound the listener hears is one voice, one guitar note ringing out, and silence.

section_notes.verse_1: DYNAMIC: pp–p. TONE: Husky, conversational, close-mic intimacy. Begin almost speaking the first line ('The porch light's pulling moths against the screen') — let the melody emerge from speech rather than being imposed on it. Place the voice in the lower-mid register (A3 area), leaning into chest voice with minimal projection. Bluegrass grain on 'screen' (slight nasal lean, let the vowel sit high and thin), 'jars' (open the R with a gentle Appalachian curl), and 'chest' (land the CH with soft aspiration, let 'est' ring). 'Your mother's singing something only summer means' should be delivered with a small, private smile audible in the tone — the corners of the mouth literally turning up, which brightens the vowels naturally. 'The kids are catching fireflies in mason jars' is a visual line — slow it down by 5%, let each image have space, micro-pause before 'in mason jars' as if watching it happen in real time. 'And the yard feels bigger underneath the stars' — open the vowel on 'stars' and give it a gentle upward slide, the first moment of awe. The closing couplet shifts from observation to feeling: 'I got dirt on my hands and peace in my chest' — deliver 'dirt' and 'peace' with equal weight, two sides of the same life; 'And this whole spinning world has come to rest' — decelerate naturally, let 'rest' land on a long, settled exhale, the note hanging as the fingerpicking pattern resolves. No vibrato in this section except the faintest natural wobble on 'rest.'
section_notes.pre_chorus: DYNAMIC: p–mp. TONE: The voice begins to lift from conversational to melodic commitment. This section is not in the provided lyrics as a separate block — it is implied in the musical transition (8 bars of F#m7–Dsus2–A–E) between verse and chorus. If vocalizing here, treat it as an instrumental swell with perhaps a hummed or 'mmm' tone from the lead, mouth closed, adding gentle melodic foreshadowing of the chorus melody. The hum should crescendo across the 8 bars, arriving at the chorus threshold with the mouth opening on the final E chord like a breath before a declaration. If this section remains instrumental, the singer should use this space to take two deep, audible breaths — the sound of gathering courage — with the second breath landing precisely on beat 4 of bar 8, launching directly into 'Let the good stay burning.' The breath itself is part of the performance.
section_notes.chorus: DYNAMIC: mp–mf (Chorus 1) / mf–f (Chorus 2). TONE: The voice transitions from Tyminski-grain to Aloe Blacc warmth. Vowels round out, chest voice engages fully, the sound projects forward with gentle authority. 'Let the good stay burning' — 'Let' is a downbeat declaration, not a plea; 'good' gets a slight dynamic push and a rounder, warmer vowel (think 'goood,' not clipped); 'burning' carries the first real vibrato of the song, slow and wide, blooming across two beats. 'Let the reel keep turning' — mirror the phrasing of the first line but with 'reel' delivered slightly higher in pitch and brighter in color, a cinematic word that should shimmer. 'We're alive inside a moment that the years will ache to hold' — THIS IS THE CORE LINE. 'Alive' should feel like the word itself is breathing; 'moment' is intimate, pulled back slightly in volume; 'years will ache' is the emotional crest — lean into 'ache' with a slight vocal push, a controlled cry, the vowel stretched and thinned as if the sound itself is longing; 'to hold' resolves with warmth, a return to chest. 'Let the porch light flicker / Let the dark come slower' — these are paired prayers, delivered with eyes-closed reverence, legato and connected. 'I'm standing in the center of the best thing that I know' — a declarative statement of faith; 'standing' is grounded and strong, 'center' is the gravitational word (give it micro-emphasis), 'best thing that I know' crescendos gently to the section's emotional resolution. 'And the good's still burning gold' — the word 'gold' is the single most important vocal moment of each chorus. Approach it with a scoop from just below pitch (a bluegrass ornament), land on it with full chest-mix resonance, and sustain with slow vibrato that decays naturally like a bell. In Chorus 2, 'gold' should be held a full beat longer than Chorus 1, with the harmony stack swelling underneath, creating the song's sonic and emotional zenith.
section_notes.verse_2: DYNAMIC: p–mp. TONE: Warmer and slightly more present than Verse 1 — the singer has settled into the story and is more emotionally invested. The subtle bass walk underneath gives the vocal permission to sit a touch higher in the register (B3–C#4 area) and push forward. 'Your daughter's got your grandma's crooked grin' — deliver 'crooked grin' with audible affection, the bluegrass curl returning on the R in 'crooked,' a small laugh almost escaping on 'grin.' 'She's spinning in the grass like a prayer on the wind' — this is the most poetic line in the song; treat 'prayer' and 'wind' as sacred words, sustaining each a fraction longer than natural speech would allow, letting the simile land with spiritual weight. 'The radio's playing from the open truck' — conversational again, tossed off casually, a scene detail. 'And every note sounds holy, every chord sounds struck' — here the voice should swell: 'holy' gets a soulful push (Aloe Blacc creeping in), 'struck' lands like a hammer on an anvil, consonant-forward, definitive. The final couplet is the emotional hinge of the entire song: 'I wanna press my thumb into the face of the clock' — deliver with building intensity, almost through gritted teeth, the desperation of a man who loves something so much he wants to physically stop time; let the voice get slightly rougher, more grain, more air; 'press my thumb' should feel tactile, like you can hear the pressure. 'And tell the hours you can go ahead and stop' — the most conversational line in the song paradoxically hits hardest; deliver it almost as spoken word over the chord change, then let 'stop' hang with a sudden, clean cutoff — no vibrato, no trail, just silence for a half-beat before the pre-chorus begins. That silence is the thumb on the clock.
section_notes.bridge: DYNAMIC: pp–ppp. TONE: The most emotionally exposed moment of the performance. Strip everything. Voice and guitar only — the voice should sound like it is coming from inside a memory, slightly reverberant (room sound, not studio plate), fragile. 'One day I'll close my eyes and I'll be right back here' — future tense that sounds like past tense, the temporal collapse audible in a voice that thins to its most vulnerable state. Deliver in a near-whisper, the melody carried more by pitch than by volume. 'The smoke, the laughter, and the sound of no fear' — this is a list of treasures. Pause after 'smoke' (one beat), after 'laughter' (one beat), then 'and the sound of no fear' is delivered as a single revelatory phrase, the voice gaining a tiny amount of strength on 'no fear' as if naming something precious. 'And I'll tell the ones I love what I should've said then' — regret and resolve in equal measure. 'The ones I love' should carry the gentlest swell of the bridge, the voice opening briefly to mp before pulling back. 'What I should've said then' has a slight vocal crack on 'should've' — not performed, but allowed, the emotion leaking through technique. 'That I saw it — every bit of it — amen' — CRITICAL MOMENT. 'That I saw it' is delivered with clear, present-tense conviction despite the future framing. ONE FULL BEAT OF SILENCE after 'it.' Then 'every bit of it' with a trembling emphasis on 'every,' the voice almost unable to contain the weight of the memory. ONE FULL BEAT OF SILENCE. Then 'amen' — ppp, barely vocalized, more breath than tone, the 'a' open and aching, the 'men' closing the mouth like the end of a prayer, with the slightest upward inflection as if it is both a statement and a question. Hold silence for TWO FULL BEATS after 'amen' before the outro guitar enters. This silence is sacred. Do not fill it.
section_notes.outro: DYNAMIC: pp with gentle rise to p, then decrescendo to ppp. TONE: A voice returning from the raw vulnerability of the bridge, now carrying a calm, knowing warmth — the sound of acceptance. The tense shift to past ('we were golden') is the emotional key: deliver it not with sadness but with profound, peaceful gratitude, as if the singer has made peace with impermanence and found that the making-peace is itself a kind of gold. 'So let the good stay burning' — the 'So' is a gentle restart, a breath after the bridge silence, the voice coming back online like a lamp being relit. Deliver at pp with a faint smile. 'Let the reel keep turning' — legato, warm, matching Chorus 1 energy but with more tenderness and less declaration. 'We were golden, we were golden' — this repeated phrase is a mantra. First iteration at p, open and full, letting the past tense land with its bittersweetness. Second iteration softer, slower, the voice beginning to pull away, as if the singer is walking backward from the scene, watching it recede. The harmony joins on the first 'golden' and fades before the second, leaving the lead exposed. 'And the good's still burning' — p, present tense returning like a small defiance, voice steady but thin. A brief ritardando in the guitar underneath. Then the final, whispered repetition: '(softly) And the good's still burning' — ppp, almost subvocalized, the words dissolving into breath, 'burning' trailing off without a clean ending, as if the voice becomes smoke. The last guitar notes ring, slow, and fade. The song ends not with a period but with an ellipsis.

minimax_prompt.lyrics:
[Intro]
(Fingerpicked guitar, gentle and luminous, 4 bars)

[Verse 1]
The porch light's pulling moths against the screen
Your mother's singing something only summer means
The kids are catching fireflies in mason jars
And the yard feels bigger underneath the stars
I got dirt on my hands and peace in my chest
And this whole spinning world has come to rest

[Pre-Chorus]
(Gentle instrumental swell, hummed tone building)

[Chorus]
Let the good stay burning
Let the reel keep turning
We're alive inside a moment that the years will ache to hold
Let the porch light flicker
Let the dark come slower
I'm standing in the center of the best thing that I know
And the good's still burning gold

[Verse 2]
Your daughter's got your grandma's crooked grin
She's spinning in the grass like a prayer on the wind
The radio's playing from the open truck
And every note sounds holy, every chord sounds struck
I wanna press my thumb into the face of the clock
And tell the hours you can go ahead and stop

[Chorus]
Let the good stay burning
Let the reel keep turning
We're alive inside a moment that the years will ache to hold
Let the porch light flicker
Let the dark come slower
I'm standing in the center of the best thing that I know
And the good's still burning gold

[Bridge]
One day I'll close my eyes and I'll be right back here
The smoke, the laughter, and the sound of no fear
And I'll tell the ones I love what I should've said then:
That I saw it — every bit of it — amen

[Outro]
So let the good stay burning
Let the reel keep turning
We were golden, we were golden
And the good's still burning
(softly)
And the good's still burning
minimax_prompt.vocal_style: Raw bluegrass tenor with soulful warmth, intimate and breathy in verses, full-chested and gently powerful in choruses, emotionally vulnerable and near-whispered in the bridge, with natural Appalachian vowel coloring, restrained vibrato on sustained notes, and a familial 3-part harmony stack on choruses that sounds human and slightly imperfect

additional_params.voice_character: Male bluegrass-soul tenor blending Dan Tyminski raw grain with Aloe Blacc round warmth, deeply earnest and emotionally present, aged roughly late-30s, voice carries natural grit and tenderness in equal measure
additional_params.harmony_style: Intimate family-choir harmonies: high third (warm female or high male) and low fifth (earthy baritone) join lead on choruses in a slightly imperfect 3-part stack, swelling to brief 4-part on Chorus 2 climax, stripped to bare solo voice on bridge, and gentle two-part on outro fading to solo lead on final line
additional_params.instrumentation_note: Travis-style fingerpicked acoustic guitar in verses and bridge, open folk strum in choruses, subtle bass walk added in verse 2, full warmth in chorus 2, ritardando in final 4 bars of outro, sparse and organic throughout
additional_params.dynamic_arc: pp intimate intro and verse 1, gentle mp build through pre-chorus, mf bloom in chorus 1 holding back 20 percent, slightly fuller verse 2, full-hearted forte peak in chorus 2, sharp drop to pp exposed voice-and-guitar bridge, ppp on amen with silence, gentle pp outro fading to ppp whispered final line
additional_params.production_feel: Golden hour warmth, close-mic'd and organic, room reverb not plate, analog warmth, no auto-tune, human breath audible, campfire intimacy
